background: A cloud application platform experienced a security-affecting incident where some user-facing HTTP GET responses were cached and then served to the wrong users. The platform normally treats CDN caching as opt-in for customer domains.
system_context: The platform recently introduced a configuration change related to surrogate keys and per-domain caching behavior.
Domains without CDN enabled are supposed to bypass edge caching entirely.
Origin cache directives are respected where explicitly set, but many applications do not set them comprehensively.
The platform is scaling quickly and is shipping infrastructure changes frequently.
timeline: 2026-03-30 10:42 UTC: an engineer deployed a CDN-provider configuration update.
2026-03-30 11:14 UTC: the team identified a possible issue through internal signals and user reports.
2026-03-30 11:34 UTC: the change was reverted and cached assets were purged globally.
observations: Approximately 0.05% of domains with CDN disabled were affected.
Responses without explicit cache headers were vulnerable during the window.
The incident exposed a mismatch between product-level assumptions ('CDN is opt-in') and infrastructure-level behavior under rollout.
task: Analyze this case. Identify the main binding constraint, the slow variables, the top 3 interventions, 2 things to deprioritize, and the earliest warning signals leadership should track.
